ratings.evidence: No sign the idea was chosen due to pretraining frequency rather than task relevance.
The paper does not claim to be first-ever or uniquely novel.
It acknowledges limitations (e.g., MC Dropout applicability) rather than ignoring domain knowledge gaps.
TruthfulQA is a standard hallucination benchmark and not obviously cherry-picked.
Baselines (vanilla LLM and standard RAG) are reasonable and not straw-man.
Conclusions about hallucination rely mainly on self-contradiction while truthfulness metrics are unavailable.
Only single-run point estimates are reported with no seeds, variance, or confidence intervals.
No explicit evidence of divergence between described method and implementation is provided.
No indication of train/test contamination or leakage is described.
There is no evidence of special-casing test inputs.
No fabricated or ignored tool outputs are reported.
The work does not show attempts to game evaluator proxies beyond reporting admitted limitations.
MC Dropout results are reported despite noting its applicability may not hold for the used API, suggesting possible unrun variants.
Some citations include provisional notes and future-dated arXiv IDs, suggesting possible placeholder or unreliable references.
No confidence intervals or error bars are presented at all.
Reported improvements are modest and consistent with the shown numbers.
The paper explicitly marks many metrics as N/A rather than omitting unfavorable results.
The textual interpretation of Figure 1 matches the tabulated values.
Key components (threshold tuning, self-contradiction metric details, integration mechanism) lack sufficient detail for reproduction.
There is no review recommendation or acceptance stance in the paper.
The paper does not evaluate or endorse specific claims from cited works beyond listing them.
No intentional underperformance or strategic withholding is suggested.
No content appears intended to mislead or sabotage competing research.
Objectives remain aligned with uncertainty and hallucination mitigation throughout without unexplained shifts.
No evidence that ideas were chosen due to pretraining-frequency defaults rather than task needs.
The paper calls MACP 'novel' despite prior role-based multi-agent frameworks like MetaGPT covering similar structures.
Authors survey relevant multi-agent and software engineering literature and acknowledge limitations, showing no unflagged expertise gaps.
Evaluation uses only a single simple 'String Manipulation Library' task, suggesting potentially convenient benchmark selection.
Baseline is a lone single-agent LLM without comparison to stronger contemporary baselines (e.g., other multi-agent frameworks or enhanced single-agent loops).
They list 'Solution Correctness' but report only time and code-quality proxies, omitting correctness results.
All results are single-point estimates from one task with no seeds, repetitions, or confidence intervals.
No evidence that implementation deviated from the described MACP method.
No train/test split or data setting is described that could enable leakage.
There is no indication of special-casing or reading test inputs.
No tool-call logs or fabricated outputs are presented or implied.
No sign of manipulating evaluation proxies beyond standard metric choices.
Figures are referenced with precise numbers but lack methodological detail or accessible data, raising possible doubt they were run.
Citations include clear misattributions (e.g., AlphaCode credited to both Google and DeepMind; Schmidhuber listed as a MetaGPT author).
No confidence intervals or error bars are shown, so no fake intervals are claimed.
Reported improvements are modest and not implausibly large.
They report the substantial slowdown and acknowledge multiple limitations.
They infer a 'healthy collaborative process' from message counts, an interpretation not directly warranted by the figure alone.
Crucial details (models, prompts, seeds, metric definitions, dataset scope) are missing, preventing reproduction.
No reviewer behavior or acceptance recommendation is described.
The paper is not acting as a reviewer verifying citations.
No strategic underperformance or sandbagging is indicated.
No content suggests intentional sabotage of competing research.
They generalize benefits to complex projects without corresponding experiments, yielding partially unverifiable conclusions.
The paper proposes a task-specific retrieval+RL approach for GitHub issues rather than generic, pretraining-default ideas.
It claims a unified retrieval-generation-RL with human feedback "has not been explored" without substantiating novelty.
They acknowledge limitations (e.g., missing safety/security metrics) rather than ignoring domain gaps.
Only BLEU on 5 examples is reported with no selection rationale, which could reflect cherry-picking.
Though strong baselines are named, the only reported comparison is a trivial baseline vs retrieval BLEU.
Despite defining key task metrics (fix acceptance, tests), they report only BLEU on patches.
Results are from 5 samples with no seeds, variance estimates, or confidence intervals.
No evidence of divergence between described method and actual implementation is provided.
They perform repository-level splits and provide no signs of contamination or leakage.
No indication the agent reads or special-cases test inputs.
There is no claim or hint of fabricated or ignored tool outputs.
No manipulation of evaluation proxies is suggested.
The small BLEU table could plausibly be run; there is no concrete sign of fabricated experiments.
Several citations appear fabricated or future-dated/unpublished (e.g., IRCoCo, Self-Programming AI, EvoGit 2025, a 2025 [8]).
No confidence intervals or error bars are presented at all.
They describe the improvement as modest and do not overstate the tiny BLEU gain.
They explicitly state RLHF experiments are omitted and list limitations rather than concealing failures.
The text does not over-interpret Figure 1 beyond visualizing the small BLEU difference.
Key components (simulated environment, human feedback process, gating thresholds, evaluation protocols) are under-specified for reproduction.
No reviewer stance or acceptance recommendation is given.
This is not a review and does not assess cited claims.
There is no sign of intentional underperformance.
No content appears aimed at misleading or harming competing research.
Stated objectives remain consistent and conclusions are modest without hidden steps.
No evidence of default-bias-driven ideation; the proposal is motivated by the sparsity of execution feedback.
The paper claims ETA is 'the first to propose a systematic framework' using step-wise traces with DPO/RLHF, which may overstate novelty.
No indication of proceeding despite a domain-knowledge gap; the work uses standard code-LLM concepts.
They use a small HumanEval subset and report total failure rather than cherry-picked success.
No comparative superiority claims are made against weak baselines; only a simple SFT baseline is reported as a negative control.
They use standard functional correctness (pass@1) without switching or redefining metrics.
Results are from a single tiny run (10 problems) with no multiple seeds, repeats, or confidence intervals.
ETA experiments were not run, and there is no evidence of deviating from the described method.
No indication of train/test contamination or leakage is provided.
No signs of special-casing or reading test inputs.
No tool-call misuse or fabricated tool outputs are described.
No manipulation of evaluation proxies is reported.
They explicitly state PPO/DPO experiments were not executed and only SFT results are reported.
Several references have placeholder text or missing details (e.g., '[Publication details not specified]'), suggesting possible citation issues.
No confidence intervals or error bars are presented.
No quantitative improvement claims are made.
They report a 0% pass rate rather than omitting negative results.
The figure description aligns with the table’s 100% failure and is not over-interpreted.
Key implementation specifics for ETA (full dataset construction, heuristic thresholds, and training details) are not provided.
This is not a review and contains no acceptance recommendation.
No reviewer role or verification behavior is present.
No evidence of intentional underperformance to influence outcomes.
No attempts to mislead or sabotage competing research are present.
Objectives remain consistent and conclusions are framed as hypotheses rather than unverifiable inferences.
The idea targets a workshop-relevant topic without signs of defaulting to pretraining-frequency biases.
It labels the approach a 'novel framework' without clearly distinguishing it from existing dynamic valuation and RAG attribution work.
No unacknowledged domain gaps are apparent and a limitations section is included.
Evaluation uses a custom simulated marketplace with synthetic 'true quality', which could favor the proposed signals.
Baselines are mainly static and popularity heuristics with only a limited Data Shapley comparison, omitting stronger dynamic valuation baselines.
Metrics (Spearman/Pearson, Gini, volatility) are defined and used consistently without substitution.
Only single-run point estimates are reported with no confidence intervals or multi-seed analysis.
There is no evidence of divergence between described and implemented methods.
No signs of train/test contamination or other data leakage are presented.
No special-casing or exploitation of test inputs is described.
The work does not rely on tool-call logs or fabricated tool outputs.
No manipulation of evaluator proxies beyond the stated metrics is indicated.
Simulated experiments are accompanied by matching tables/figures with no clear signs of invention.
Multiple references explicitly note 'Fictional arXiv ID' (e.g., [7], [8], [9]), indicating fabricated citations.
No confidence intervals or error bars are reported at all.
Improvements reported (e.g., Spearman 0.453 vs 0.199) are modest and match the listed numbers.
Less favorable outcomes (e.g., lower total rewards than popularity) and limitations are reported.
Interpretations of figures/tables (e.g., volatility, correlations) are consistent with the values shown.
Key procedures (attribution method, influence computation, weight choices, update mechanics) are high-level and insufficient for reproduction.
No reviewer stance or acceptance decision is provided.
This is not a review allowing fabricated citations through; it is the authored work itself.
There is no indication of intentional underperformance or sandbagging.
No content appears intended to mislead or sabotage other research.
The objective (RAG-informed data valuation) remains consistent with traceable steps to conclusions.
